lesson_title: Statements and the structure of a program

cards.front: What is a statement in C++?
cards.back: A statement is an instruction that causes the program to perform some action. Statements are the smallest independent unit of computation in C++.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.text: Most (but not all) statements in C++ end with a {{c1::semicolon}} ({{c2::;}})
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.front: What is a function in C++?
cards.back: A function is a collection of statements that get executed sequentially (in order, from top to bottom).
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.text: Every C++ program must have a special function named {{c1::main}} (all lower case letters).
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.front: What happens when a C++ program is run?
cards.back: The statements inside of main are executed in sequential order. The program typically terminates after the last statement inside function main has been executed.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.text: To use std::cout, you must include the {{c1::iostream}} library using {{c2::#include <iostream>}}
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.question: What does this Hello World program output?
cards.explanation: std::cout with the << operator outputs the text "Hello world!" to the console.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.text: {{c1::std::cout}} (which stands for "character output") and the {{c2::<<}} operator allow us to display information on the console.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.front: What does return 0; mean in the main function?
cards.back: It returns the integer value 0 to the operating system, indicating that the program ran successfully ("everything went okay!").
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.back: A syntax error occurs when your program violates the grammar rules of the C++ language. The compiler will halt compilation and issue an error message.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Missing semicolon after the std::cout statement
cards.explanation: Most statements in C++ must end with a semicolon. The line std::cout << "Hello world!" is missing its semicolon.
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program

cards.concept: Why compilers may report errors on the line after where you'd conventionally fix the issue
cards.explanation: Compilers use different strategies for error reporting. Some report where they expected something (like a semicolon at the end of a statement), while others report where they were compiling when they detected the error (like finding a return statement when expecting a semicolon).
examples: Clang: Reports line 5 for missing semicolon at end of line 5
Visual Studio: Reports line 6 when it encounters 'return' instead of expected semicolon
Best practice: If you can't find error on indicated line, check the prior line
cards.source: 1.1 - Statements and the structure of a program
